items.title: Show HN: Shikigami, run AI coding agents in parallel, each in a Git worktree
items.why_it_matters: An indie developer built Shikigami, a desktop app that runs multiple AI coding sessions in parallel, each isolated in its own Git worktree so agents don't overwrite each other's code. This is perfect for your workflow because it lets you run multiple Claude or Codex sessions at once while keeping your AI-ingest repo clean. Try downloading Shikigami this week and running two parallel sessions—one for a UI tweak and one for a backend bug—to see how it manages your Git branches.

items.title: I built llms.txt files to make local businesses discoverable by AI agents
items.why_it_matters: An indie developer built a project using llms.txt files to make local businesses discoverable by AI agents, showing a practical way to build for the agentic web. You can use this same pattern to make your own projects AI-friendly. Try creating a basic llms.txt file for AI-ingest today and hosting it on your Vercel deployment so other AI tools can easily read and understand your project.

items.title: Show HN: Agentic code review on PRs for less than $1/each
items.why_it_matters: An open-source tool lets you run agentic code reviews on your GitHub Pull Requests for less than a dollar per run. Since you are currently learning proper Git and commit discipline on the AI-ingest repo, this is a cheap way to get professional-grade feedback on your code. Try setting up this GitHub Action on your AI-ingest repository this week to automatically review your next feature branch before you merge it.

items.title: Anthropic extends Claude Code's 50% weekly limit increase through August 19
items.why_it_matters: Anthropic has extended the 50% weekly limit increase for Claude Code through August 19, giving you more runway to build without hitting usage caps. This means you can rely heavily on your primary coding tool without worrying about running out of prompts mid-session. Try kicking off a larger refactoring session on AI-ingest today using Claude Code, knowing you have the extra limit buffer to finish it.

items.title: Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now
items.why_it_matters: Claude Code has been updated to run on Bun (written in Rust), significantly speeding up the startup and execution times of your primary terminal coding tool. This directly reduces the friction of waiting for your agent to initialize and run commands. Try running `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@latest` in your terminal today to instantly speed up your AI-ingest development sessions.

items.title: Compiled List of over $1000 in AI/GPU/LLM Credits (100% Free)
items.why_it_matters: A curated list of over $1,000 in free AI, GPU, and LLM credits has been compiled, offering free access to APIs and hosting platforms. As a beginner building on a budget, this is an easy way to offset your API costs for AI-ingest or future side projects. Try visiting the directory today and claiming one of the Supabase or LLM provider credits to fund your database or API usage this week.

items.title: Show HN: Claude Code Workflows for GPT/Codex
items.why_it_matters: An open-source project introduces Claude Code-style terminal workflows for GPT and Codex, allowing you to use similar agentic commands with alternative models. Since you already use Codex alongside Claude, this lets you bring your terminal-based workflow to other APIs. Try cloning the repo this week and running a test prompt using your Codex API key to see if it fits your vibe-coding setup.
